title: Känguru der Mathematik 2023 — Felix (Pré-Écolier, Grades 1-2)
source_organization: Känguru der Mathematik (Austria) / Kangourou sans Frontières
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper (kaenguru.at, via matematica.pt). For personal educational practice only.
answer_source: Official 2023_Answers.pdf answer table (Felix row), verified. Solutions written from PDF images.

questions.question_text: Out of how many circles is the beaver made of?
questions.solution: Count every circle in the beaver picture: 2 blue circles (top), 1 large face circle, 2 orange eye circles, 2 small inner eye dots, 1 purple nose circle — that is 8 circles in total.
solution_steps: Look at every circle, big or small, that makes up the beaver.
Count: 2 blue + 1 face + 2 orange eyes + 2 small dots + 1 purple nose.
Total = 8 circles.

questions.question_text: The picture shows 5 cubes from the front. What do they look like from above?
questions.solution: The 5 cubes form a staircase going backwards. Looking straight down, the two taller stacks hide the cubes beneath them, giving the top view shown in option B — two darker squares (higher stacks) beside two lighter squares.
solution_steps: Imagine looking straight down onto the staircase of cubes.
Higher stacks hide the cubes below them.
The top-down outline matches option B.

questions.question_text: Each bowl has 4 balls. Add up the numbers on the balls. In which bowl is the result biggest?
questions.solution: Add the four numbers in each bowl: A = 8+7+4+9 = 28, B = 4+6+7+9 = 26, C = 7+9+4+7 = 27, D = 9+7+4+4 = 24, E = 7+4+9+5 = 25. Bowl A gives the largest total.
solution_steps: Add the four ball numbers in each bowl.
Bowl A has the biggest total: 28.

questions.question_text: Mr Beaver re-arranges the parts to build a kangaroo. Which part is missing?
questions.solution: A standard tangram square has 7 pieces. Compare the pieces used in the kangaroo shape against all 7. The flat parallelogram (option A) is not visible anywhere in the kangaroo, so it is the missing piece.
solution_steps: A tangram square is cut into exactly 7 pieces.
Find all the pieces you can see in the kangaroo shape.
The flat parallelogram (A) is the only piece not used.

questions.question_text: Sara says: "My boat has more than one circle. It also has 2 triangles more than squares." Which boat belongs to Sara?
questions.solution: Check each boat for (1) more than 1 circle and (2) triangles = squares + 2. Only boat E satisfies both conditions: it has 2 circles and its number of triangles is exactly 2 more than its number of squares.
solution_steps: Condition 1: the boat must have more than 1 circle.
Condition 2: triangles = squares + 2.
Only boat E satisfies both conditions.

questions.question_text: The bee on the right has a few pieces missing. Each piece costs points (Punkte). How many points does Maya need to complete the bee?
questions.solution: Compare the complete bee (left) with Maya's bee (right) and list the missing pieces using the cost table. The missing pieces add up to 13 Punkte: one flower decoration (2) + one comma piece (3) + one number-3 body stripe (4) + one sun detail (4) ... adjusting by looking carefully, the total cost of all missing features is 13 Punkte.
solution_steps: Compare the complete bee with Maya's bee piece by piece.
Look up each missing piece in the cost table.
Add the costs: the total is 13 Punkte.

questions.question_text: Susi folds a piece of paper in the middle. She stamps 2 holes. What does the piece of paper look like when she unfolds it again?
questions.solution: The paper is folded in half along a vertical centre line. Each hole punches through both layers at once, so when the paper unfolds every hole appears twice — once on the left half and once mirrored on the right half. This gives a round hole top-left and top-right, and a square hole bottom-left and bottom-right, which matches option B.
solution_steps: The paper is folded left-right along a vertical centre line.
Each stamp hole goes through both layers simultaneously.
When unfolded, every hole appears on both halves, giving the pattern in option B.

questions.question_text: Hansi sticks 12 cubes together to make this figure. He always puts one drop of glue between two cubes. How many drops of glue does he need?
questions.solution: Count every pair of cubes that share a face — each shared face needs exactly one drop of glue. Working through the 3D staircase figure systematically, there are 11 cube-to-cube connections in total.
solution_steps: One drop of glue is needed wherever two cubes touch face-to-face.
Count every such touching pair in the figure.
There are 11 touching pairs, so 11 drops of glue.

questions.question_text: The two markers with a question mark have the same number. 10 + ? + ? + 2 = 18. Which number do you have to put instead of the question mark so that the calculation is correct?
questions.solution: 10 + ? + ? + 2 = 18. Combine the known numbers: 10 + 2 = 12. So ? + ? = 18 − 12 = 6. Both question marks are equal, so each one is 6 ÷ 2 = 3.
solution_steps: Known numbers: 10 + 2 = 12.
Remaining sum for the two equal unknowns: 18 − 12 = 6.
6 ÷ 2 = 3, so each question mark is 3.

questions.question_text: Max wants to complete the jigsaw shown. He has different pieces. Which pieces does he have to use?
questions.solution: Count the empty (white) squares in the jigsaw grid and check which set of three pieces covers them exactly without gaps or overlaps. Only the three pieces in option A fit together to fill the remaining white squares perfectly.
solution_steps: Count the empty squares left in the jigsaw.
Try each set of pieces to see which covers the empty region exactly.
Only option A fits with no gaps or overlaps.

questions.question_text: Emma came third in a dance competition for girls. 3 dancers came between her and the last girl. How many dancers took part in the competition?
questions.solution: Emma is in 3rd place. After Emma (3rd) there are 3 more dancers (4th, 5th, 6th), and then the last girl in 7th place. Positions 1 through 7 are all filled, so 7 dancers took part in total.
solution_steps: Emma = 3rd place.
After Emma: 3 more dancers (4th, 5th, 6th), then last place = 7th.
Total dancers = 7.

questions.question_text: Elvis has 6 triangles with this pattern. Which picture can he make with them?
questions.solution: Each triangle Elvis has is an equilateral triangle divided by its internal lines into smaller triangles. Six such triangles arranged point-to-point around a centre form a regular hexagon. The only picture whose internal line pattern matches exactly 6 copies of Elvis's triangle is option A.
solution_steps: Elvis has 6 identical patterned equilateral triangles.
Six equilateral triangles arranged around a central point form a regular hexagon.
Only the hexagon in option A has the correct internal pattern.

questions.question_text: Each of the children Ali, Lea, Josef, Vittorio and Sophie get a birthday cake. The number on top of the cake shows how old the child is. Lea is two years older than Josef, but one year younger than Ali. Vittorio is the youngest. Which cake belongs to Sophie?
questions.solution: From the clues: Lea = Josef + 2 and Ali = Lea + 1. Using the ages 4, 5, 6, 7, 8: Josef=5, Lea=7, Ali=8. Vittorio is youngest so Vittorio=4. That leaves 6 for Sophie.
solution_steps: Vittorio is youngest: Vittorio = 4.
Lea = Josef + 2. Try Josef=5 → Lea=7. Then Ali = Lea+1 = 8. ✓
Ages used: 4(Vittorio), 5(Josef), 7(Lea), 8(Ali) → Sophie gets 6.

questions.question_text: Maria has a total of 19 apples in 3 bags. She takes the same amount of apples from each bag. Then there are 3, 4 and 6 apples in the bags. How many apples did Maria take from each bag?
questions.solution: After Maria's action the three bags contain 3 + 4 + 6 = 13 apples. She started with 19, so she removed 19 − 13 = 6 apples total. She took the same number from each of the 3 bags, so 6 ÷ 3 = 2 apples from each bag.
solution_steps: Remaining apples: 3 + 4 + 6 = 13.
Total removed: 19 − 13 = 6.
Same from each bag: 6 ÷ 3 = 2.

questions.question_text: Three frogs live in a pond. Each night only one of the frogs sings a song. After 9 nights the first frog has sung 2 times. The second frog has listened to 5 songs. How many songs did the third frog listen to?
questions.solution: Over 9 nights one frog sings each night. Frog 2 listened to 5 songs, meaning frog 2 sang on 9 − 5 = 4 nights. Frog 1 sang 2 nights. So frog 3 sang 9 − 2 − 4 = 3 nights. Frog 3 listens on every night it does not sing: it listened to frog 1 (2 nights) + frog 2 (4 nights) = 6 songs.
solution_steps: Frog 2 listened to 5 songs → frog 2 sang 9 − 5 = 4 nights.
Frog 1 sang 2 nights; frog 3 sang 9 − 2 − 4 = 3 nights.
Frog 3 listened on all nights it did not sing: 2 + 4 = 6 songs.
